chapter_title: Type Conversion, Type Aliases, and Type Deduction
lesson_title: Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.front: What is the key safety feature of brace initialization (list initialization) regarding type conversions?
cards.back: Brace initialization disallows narrowing conversions that would result in data loss. This prevents common bugs from implicit conversions.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.text: Brace initialization (also called {{c1::list initialization}}) disallows {{c2::narrowing conversions}}.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.question: Which of these compile and which don't?
cards.answer: Line 1 compiles (copy init allows narrowing). Lines 2 and 3 fail (brace init disallows narrowing).
cards.explanation: Copy initialization (=) allows narrowing conversions, but brace initialization ({ } or = { }) does not.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.front: When does brace initialization allow a narrowing conversion from a larger integral type to a smaller one?
cards.back: When the initializer is a constexpr AND the value fits within the range of the smaller type. The compiler can verify at compile-time that no data loss will occur.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.question: Which of these compile?
cards.explanation: Lines 1 and 3 use constexpr values that fit in char range, which is allowed. Line 2 uses a non-constexpr variable, so the compiler can't verify the value at compile-time.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.text: Brace initialization allows narrowing from a larger integral type to smaller if the initializer is a {{c1::constexpr}} and the value {{c2::fits within the target type's range}}.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.question: Why does this fail to compile?
cards.code:
constexpr int n{ 300 };
char c{ n };
cards.answer: Compile error: 300 is outside the range of char (-128 to 127 for signed char).
cards.explanation: Even though n is constexpr, the value 300 doesn't fit in a char, so brace initialization rejects this narrowing conversion.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.front: Does brace initialization allow conversions from floating point to integral types?
cards.back: No. Brace initialization disallows all conversions from floating point to integral types, even if the floating point value is constexpr and has no fractional part (like 3.0).
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.question: Why does this fail even though the value fits?
cards.code:
constexpr double d{ 3.0 };
int i{ d };
cards.answer: Compile error: Brace initialization disallows ALL conversions from floating point to integral, even constexpr ones without fractional parts.
cards.explanation: The rule is strict: no floating point to integral conversions are allowed with brace initialization, regardless of the value.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.explanation:
Brace initialization is safer than copy initialization because:
  • Disallows narrowing conversions that lose data
  • Prevents accidental data loss from implicit conversions
  • Catches potential bugs at compile-time
  • Makes intent clearer in code
This is why it's the recommended initialization form in modern C++.
examples: int y = 3.5;   // OK but loses .5 silently
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.text: The form {{c1::int x = {3.5};}} also uses brace initialization and will {{c2::disallow narrowing conversions}} just like int x{3.5}.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.front: Does brace initialization allow conversions between signed and unsigned integral types?
cards.back: Generally no, unless the initializer is a constexpr that fits within the target type's range. For example, unsigned int u{-1} fails, but unsigned int u{5} succeeds if 5 is constexpr.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.text: List initialization using {{c1::braces}} is safer than copy initialization because it {{c2::disallows narrowing conversions}}.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

cards.text: Brace initialization disallows {{c1::all}} conversions from floating point to integral, even if the value is {{c2::constexpr}}.
cards.source: 10.4 - Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers
